current_fact.fact_text: On April 10, 1972, U.S. B-52 bombers resumed bombing North Vietnam for the first time since 1967, unleashing high-altitude carpet bombing in an already devastated land. The roar of the eight-engine aircraft became a grim soundtrack to a war increasingly questioned back home.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

current_fact.match_reason: The heavy, cinematic presence of B-52 raids is reflected in the moody, filmic textures of trip-hop, a genre that often grapples with conflict, memory, and nighttime cityscapes.

queue.fact_text: In 1973, Invicta International Airlines Flight 435 crashed into a wooded hillside near Hochwald while approaching Basel in a snowstorm, killing 108 of the 145 people on board. Navigational confusion and poor weather combined to turn a routine charter flight into Switzerland’s deadliest air disaster.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A tragedy born from haste, stress, and bad conditions is answered with an episode inviting us to slow down, breathe, and move more gently through dangerous skies and busy lives.

queue.fact_text: On April 10, 2016, a magnitude 6.6 earthquake struck near Ishkashim in Afghanistan, shaking regions across India, Tajikistan, Pakistan, and Kashmir. The quake reminded millions how mountains that seem eternal are constantly shifting under our feet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: A tectonic jolt in Central Asian ranges pairs with an episode shaped by mountain landscapes, inviting us to listen to how geology and memory echo through music.

queue.fact_text: On April 10, 1963, the same day as the Thresher disaster, the sea itself was also a silent witness to decades of submarine experimentation and Cold War tension. Every sonar ping and steel hull carried stories of exploration, secrecy, and the thin line between technology and catastrophe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: The hidden world of submarines beneath the waves is complemented by an episode immersed in underwater life and Arctic dives, trading steel hulls for whale fins.

queue.fact_text: On April 10, 1963 and through the Cold War, submarine design pushed the limits of human engineering, forcing us to rethink pressure, stealth, and survival underwater. Each vessel became a kind of moving laboratory, where physics, fear, and innovation travelled together in the dark.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: The painstaking trial-and-error of building safe submarines matches an episode about the deeper process of learning, failure, and gradual mastery under pressure.

queue.fact_text: On April 10 throughout history, the Earth’s skies and oceans have been tested by storms, comets, and human machines—but animals have also navigated these changing worlds far longer than we have. From seabirds reading magnetic fields to whales communicating across oceans, their survival skills quietly outshine our technologies.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

queue.match_reason: A nod to the quiet wisdom of animals under human-altered skies fits with a forest-morning dub episode where nature, slowness, and deep listening take the lead.

queue.fact_text: Across different April 10ths, from volcanic winters to political revolutions, one thread is constant: humans adapting—learning new tools, new sounds, new ways of living together on a restless planet. Every event is another reminder that change, though unsettling, is our oldest companion.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A meta-reflection on many April 10 stories—disaster, conflict, art, and adaptation—pairs with an introspective episode about healing, transition, and looking back to move forward.

published.fact_text: In 837, Halley's Comet made its closest known approach to Earth, coming within just 0.0342 astronomical units—about 5.1 million kilometers. Medieval skywatchers would have seen an enormous, ominous streak in the heavens long before anyone knew what a comet actually was.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A cosmic close pass by Halley’s Comet pairs naturally with an episode about reimagining ‘space’ and shifting perspective.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1815, Mount Tambora began one of the most powerful volcanic eruptions in recorded history, continuing for months. The ash it hurled into the atmosphere dimmed the Sun enough to help trigger the 'Year Without a Summer' in 1816, causing crop failures and strange weather across the Northern Hemisphere.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A cataclysmic eruption that altered seasons connects with an episode themed around storms, rain, and emerging into hopeful weather.

published.fact_text: In 1991, satellites documented a rare tropical storm forming in the South Atlantic Ocean near Angola—an ocean basin where such storms almost never appear. For decades, meteorologists had debated whether South Atlantic tropical cyclones were even possible, until this storm provided clear, swirling proof from orbit.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A freak tropical storm in an almost storm-free ocean feels like nature’s own ‘electric shock’, echoed by the high-voltage rock of this episode.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1979, a massive tornado tore through Wichita Falls, Texas, as part of the Red River Valley tornado outbreak, killing 42 people in that city alone. The storm became infamous not only for its destructive power but also for the haunting images of entire neighborhoods reduced to slabs.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: Violent atmospheric motion is contrasted with an episode about slowing down and moving mindfully, turning chaotic weather into a call for grounded presence.

published.fact_text: In 1963, the nuclear-powered submarine USS Thresher sank during deep-diving tests, taking 129 sailors and shipyard personnel with her. The disaster led to a sweeping overhaul of submarine safety, including the U.S. Navy's SUBSAFE program that still shapes how undersea vessels are built and tested.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A story from the ocean’s depths pairs with an episode built on aquatic metaphors of diving, immersion, and the hidden world below the surface.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1912, the RMS Titanic left Southampton for her maiden voyage, advertised as practically unsinkable. Just days later, the ship would become a symbol of human hubris and tragedy, but on departure she was a floating palace embodying early 20th-century optimism and luxury.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A legendary ocean voyage—grand but doomed—resonates with a more intimate, modern story of life at sea and what it means to travel together by boat.

published.fact_text: In 1866, Henry Bergh founded the American Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (ASPCA) in New York City. At a time when animal welfare was barely considered, his activism helped spark a global movement to recognize animals as beings worthy of protection rather than just property.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: A pioneering act of compassion for vulnerable beings fits with an episode celebrating powerful voices who speak up, nurture, and challenge the status quo.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1970, Paul McCartney announced he was leaving The Beatles, effectively signaling the end of a band that had reshaped popular music. The breakup forced each member to confront life beyond the identity that had defined them in the public eye for a decade.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

published.match_reason: McCartney leaving The Beatles is a radical identity shift, mirrored by an episode explicitly about musical alter-egos and expanding who you can be.

published.fact_text: In 1724, Johann Sebastian Bach led the first performance of his Easter cantata "Erfreut euch, ihr Herzen" (BWV 66) in Leipzig. The work repurposed earlier music into a jubilant celebration, showing how Bach could recycle his own material into something spiritually and emotionally new.

episode.title: Sedna FM #03 - Beautiful as You Feel
episode.description: An episode about self-acceptance and inner beauty. Music that reminds us we are beautiful as we feel, embracing our authentic selves. Warm, uplifting sounds that celebrate individuality.
songs: The The - this is the day
DCTL - Deep Tannin f(eat Rafael Leafar)

published.match_reason: A joyful Easter cantata celebrating renewed life aligns with an uplifting episode about beauty, vitality, and inner celebration.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1545, the Spanish founded the mining city of Potosí in present-day Bolivia after discovering extraordinarily rich silver deposits in Cerro Rico. For centuries, this single mountain fueled European empires, global trade, and untold human suffering, becoming a symbol of both wealth and exploitation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: A pivotal chapter in Spanish colonial history connects with an episode that dives into one of Spain’s deepest musical traditions and cultural memories.

published.fact_text: In 1717, Robert Walpole resigned from the British government, triggering the 'Whig Split'—a bitter party division that reshaped British politics until 1720. Ironically, this fracture cleared the path for Walpole to return a few years later and become Britain’s first de facto prime minister.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A historic political split and reconfiguration parallels an episode about breaking with old habits and renegotiating how systems—political or digital—shape our lives.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1809, Austria launched the War of the Fifth Coalition by invading Bavaria, hoping to roll back Napoleon’s dominance in Europe. Instead, the campaign would showcase Napoleon's rapid maneuver warfare—though it also hinted that his era of invincibility was beginning to crack.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: An anti-Napoleonic coalition rising against an empire mirrors the charged, resistance-focused energy of a band explicitly raging against systems of power.

published.fact_text: In 1796, a surprise Austrian attack at the Battle of Voltri marked the beginning of Napoleon Bonaparte’s Italian Campaign, a whirlwind series of battles that would end the War of the First Coalition. The campaign turned the relatively unknown general into a European celebrity and rewrote the art of modern warfare.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: An explosive Italian campaign led by a bold strategist pairs with an Italian-language episode about trailblazing women rewriting the rules of rock.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1814, Allied forces under the Duke of Wellington attacked Toulouse, driving Marshal Soult’s French troops from the city after fierce fighting—ironically, after Napoleon had already agreed to abdicate. Soldiers on both sides risked their lives in a battle whose political outcome had effectively been decided days earlier.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: A hard-fought battle continuing beyond its political meaning resonates with an episode about martial training, discipline, and the paradox of fighting when the deeper story has already shifted.

published.fact_text: In 1868, at Arogee in Abyssinia (Ethiopia), British and Indian forces defeated Emperor Tewodros II’s army, suffering only two deaths compared to around 700 Ethiopian fatalities. The clash was part of a dramatic expedition that ended with Tewodros’s suicide and a trove of looted manuscripts scattering Ethiopian heritage across European museums.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: A colonial-era incursion into the Horn of Africa is paired with an episode amplifying contemporary Saharan voices of resistance and survival, reclaiming narrative and sound.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1919, the Third Regional Congress of Peasants, Workers and Insurgents met in Huliaipole under the Makhnovshchina, an anarchist movement in Ukraine. Delegates tried to build a radically self-managed society amid the chaos of civil war, designing structures without bosses or traditional states.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: Anarchist congresses dreaming of self-governed communities align with a politically sharp artist whose lyrics challenge power structures and imagine other ways to live.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1941, the Axis powers proclaimed the Independent State of Croatia, a Nazi-aligned puppet regime. It enacted brutal policies, including genocidal campaigns, making it a dark reminder of how quickly political borders and identities can be rewritten under authoritarian pressure.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

published.match_reason: A regime built on exclusion and violence contrasts with an episode celebrating inclusive, liberating spaces where people—especially women—claim room and dignity.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1972, U.S. B-52 bombers resumed bombing North Vietnam for the first time since 1967, unleashing high-altitude carpet bombing in an already devastated land. The roar of the eight-engine aircraft became a grim soundtrack to a war increasingly questioned back home.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: The heavy, cinematic presence of B-52 raids is reflected in the moody, filmic textures of trip-hop, a genre that often grapples with conflict, memory, and nighttime cityscapes.
